course_description:
This course introduces students to studio-based learning and serves as an introduction to the Design Computing studio stream. Working in small multidisciplinary teams, students will conduct research, analyse data, and propose user-centred design solutions, and present evidence-based rationales to justify their design decisions. Students will also program design artifacts to collect additional data to inform and validate their solutions. Finally, this course will also enable students to develop important skills such as problem solving, reflection in action, and communication.

Students will be introduced to methods and skills for the generation, refinement, and implementation of their design, including critical reflection, project scoping, and analysis. Students will develop the ability to communicate design ideas effectively, identify different design approaches, and assess the impact and outcomes of these approaches on both their users and society as a whole. Additionally, they will gain skills in managing team-based work, including conflict resolution and effective collaboration skills. These skills will be applicable in future studio subjects, including Studio 2 and Studio 3. At the end of the semester, students will present their work to teaching staff and peers at a final tradeshow. Studio 1 expects all students to be able to undertake both design and programming tasks.

DECO7180 has been reworked based on previous student feedback to make it more in-line with studio-based learning, and to provide more guidance each week on what tasks students should be undertaking.

companions: You'll need to complete the following courses at the same time:
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course has 5 scheduled contact hours per student each week.
Alongside that, you can expect to spend about five additional hours on your own work.
Studio (3 hours)—
provides a space for students to work collaboratively with their teams and peers, focusing on both theory and practical design-related content.
Studios commence in week 1.
Practical Class (2 hours)—
in class activities designed to provide guidance on the design process, as well as discussion around weekly activities.
Practicals commence in week 1.
Self-study (5 hours)—
Students are expected to continue work outside of scheduled Studio and Practical times on course activities, weekly check-ins, and self-directed learning related to both the project and individual assessments.
End of Semester Tradeshow
Teams will demonstrate their final projects at an in-studio tradeshow. Individual teams will all be required to be present for the entire 3 hours of the in-studio tradeshow. This is an in-person tradeshow for all students in the DECO7180 course. Please ensure this date is in your diary and you have cleared all other commitments.

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse design concepts and their implications by examining underlying design principles and evaluating their effectiveness in practical applications.
Apply critical thinking to reflect on and analyse the design’s impact and outcomes, evaluating how well they align with user needs.
Create an argument to defend design decisions by integrating theoretical concepts, user feedback, and qualitative and quantitative data to support a compelling design rationale.
Apply foundational knowledge in design principles, theories, and methods within a studio environment and engage in iterative development processes to enhance design outcomes.
Understand ethical considerations in design decisions and apply critical analysis to assess how design choices impact user experiences and cultural contexts.
Apply collaborative tools and techniques to work effectively with team members, clearly communicate design decisions, and document progress to convey design ideas and track development.

assessment_details.title: Problem Framing Report
assessment_details.task_description: This report asks you to present an evidence-based argument that communicates an understanding of the problem space that you are designing for. Working in teams, you will receive and analyse two datasets, developing a clear and coherent argument relating to a potential design direction. You will identify and defend key design insights, propose potential design directions, outline user concerns related to the problem space, and evaluate the likely impact of specific design choices. The written report will be submitted to Blackboard as a team assessment in PDF or DOCX format. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission is on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
This course uses team-based assessment. Extensions will require a single request submitted with agreement from at least 50% of the members of the group, and recognition of potential impacts on the other group members. This assessment forms a crucial part of the students’ iterative design process, allowing them to articulate their design rationale and propose actionable design strategies. Staff feedback aims to guide students, helping them identify and correct any misconceptions while outlining potential next steps. Therefore, the maximum extension has been capped at 14 days so that feedback can be provided and to ensure that students remain on track in the iterative design process.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.due_date: 3/03/2026 - 4/03/2026 10/03/2026 - 11/03/2026 17/03/2026 - 18/03/2026 24/03/2026 - 25/03/2026 31/03/2026 - 1/04/2026 14/04/2026 - 15/04/2026 21/04/2026 - 22/04/2026 28/04/2026 - 29/04/2026 5/05/2026 - 6/05/2026 12/05/2026 - 13/05/2026 19/05/2026 - 20/05/2026 25/05/2026 - 8/06/2026 Weekly Activities are to be submitted on Blackboard before your scheduled practical. assessment_details.task_description: Over the course of the semester, you will be working in teams to develop an interactive technology prototype based on an assigned pair of datasets. You will be asked to complete weekly design activities, conduct user research, interrogate design decisions and their potential implications, and document your design reasoning and individual contributions to the team project in a clear, structured manner. These design decisions will be discussed and explored throughout your weekly practical classes. This is a sequenced assessment, meaning that you will be asked to submit your work weekly. This will occur before the start of each weekly practical class. These tasks will revolve around specific question sets, scenarios, or design challenges. These will be released weekly on Blackboard. This sequence culminates in an identity verified in-person interview, which will be scheduled in Week 13 and Exam Week 1. During this interview, you will draw upon your weekly submissions as stimulus material to discuss, justify, and defend your individual design decisions and contributions over the course of the semester. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Weekly online activities: To be completed on Blackboard before weekly your weekly practical In Class Discussion: To be undertaken in your practical. Each weekly activity concludes with an in-class discussion, and completing the online component is essential for participation. Interview: Will take place in Week 13 and Exam Week 1. This will be recorded.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Weekly Activities: Because only the best 9 of 11 will contribute to the mark for this assessment item and results/answers are released soon after the due date, no extensions are permitted.
Interview: Will take place in Week 13 and Exam Week 1.
If there are exceptional circumstances, please reach out to studentenquiries@eecs.uq.edu.au with supporting documentation.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Weekly Activities: Because the results/answers are released soon after the due date and only the best 9 of 11 will contribute to the mark for this assessment item a 100% penalty will be applied to late submission.
Interview: 100% Late Penalty (no grace period) as it is a secured identify verified assessment and a Pass/Fail hurdle for the sequence.

assessment_details.title: Team Collaboration Showcase
assessment_details.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Presentation, Product/ Design
assessment_details.task_description: Over the course of this semester you will be working with a team to create design artifacts as part of your exploration of a design space. The final outcome of this iterative design process will be a dynamic interactive website, based on your initial problem framing. The outcome of this assessment will showcase the culmination of your collaborative teamwork and the design justifications made throughout the semester. During the tradeshow you will be asked to demonstrate how your team has collaborated over the course of multiple iterative design cycles, with the final design artifact serving as evidence of your process. Your submission will include the code-base for your website, records of contributions made by individual team members and supporting documentation to be submitted before the exhibit. This supporting documentation will serve as evidence for your team's contributions. Your demonstration will be graded by two examiners and a post-marking moderation process will be applied. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The demonstration will take place during the exhibit, which will be scheduled during studio in Week 13. The final Design Artifact (code and supporting documentation) will be submitted to Blackboard before the studio starts and will be used as part of your team demonstration.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions for groupwork are typically not available as this impacts on all members of the team.
Students with valid extension requests either receive team mark or will be required to undertake alternative assessment.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Due to the nature of the demonstration taking place during the exhibit, there will be a 100% late penalty if the team does not present during the scheduled studio session. This is consistent with industry practice around presentations to clients/industry partners.

assessment_details.title: Project Impact Report
assessment_details.task_description: An important skill-set for designers is to be able to think about the implications of their work. This purpose of this assessment is to offer students the opportunity to think critically about how specific design decisions, features, and interactions impact end users - ethically, culturally, socially, etc. In this report, you will apply data collected throughout the semester along with both academic and non-academic research to outline and define the potential impact of your design decisions, what negative impacts or harms you're willing to accept, and why. This is intended to be both personal and reflective and will draw upon your weekly activities as a source of evidence. This report will be submitted via Blackboard in a PDF or DOCX format. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Studios Studios introduce both theoretical and practical design content, which students explore and apply first through team projects. Support is provided for project work, including assessments, project-based presentations, and exercises. This culminates in the final in-studio tradeshow presentations. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Applied Classes Applied Classes offer activities and tasks that help guide students in developing their skill-set as a designer. These sessions are intended to provide scaffolding to the design process and to get students to reflect on their design choices. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
